elements.sent-2411-001: We present a comprehensive analysis of \HI\ absorption around 96 \lya\ emitters (LAEs) at $z\approx3.3$ (median \lya\ luminosity $\approx10^{42}$ erg.s$^{-1}$).
elements.sent-2411-002: These LAEs were identified within 8 MUSE fields, each $1'\times1'$ on the sky and centered on a bright background quasar, as part of the MUSEQuBES survey.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using Voigt profile fitting for all \HI\ absorbers detected within $\pm500$~\kms\ of these LAEs, we compiled a catalog of 800 \HI\ absorption components.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our analysis shows that \HI\ absorption is enhanced near the LAEs compared to the IGM.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, no trend is found between the column densities of \HI\ absorbers and their impact parameters from the LAEs (spanning $\approx54$–260 pkpc).
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, all galaxies associated with Lyman-limit systems have impact parameters $>50$~pkpc from the quasar sightlines, suggesting that true absorber-hosts may be too faint to detect.
elements.sent-2411-007: The LAEs show an overall \HI\ covering fraction (\fc(\HI)) of $\approx88\%$ for a threshold \logN(\HI)$~=15$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Notably, at the same threshold, the pairs/group LAEs exhibit a $100\%$ \HI\ covering fraction out to $\approx 250$~pkpc.
elements.sent-2411-009: In contrast, isolated LAEs consistently show a lower \fc(\HI) of $\approx80\%$.
elements.sent-2411-010: This environmental influence on \fc(\HI) is also evident up to $\approx 300$~\kms\ in differential bins of line-of-sight velocity.
elements.sent-2411-011: We find an anti-correlation between \fc(\HI) and the rest-frame \lya-emission equivalent width (\ew).
elements.sent-2411-012: Based on the \lya-shell model, this could imply that gas-rich galaxies tend to reside in gas-rich environments or that the higher \ew\ LAEs are more efficient at ionizing their surrounding medium.
elements.sent-2411-013: The circumgalactic medium (CGM) is a dynamic, complex, multiphase gaseous region, extending out to $\sim$few~100~kpc, enveloping galaxies \citep[see the review by][]{Tumlinson2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: It contains the imprints of crucial processes such as galactic-scale winds and the accretion of intergalactic baryons via the ``hot mode''/``cold mode'' along cosmic filaments \citep{Keres_2005, van_de_voort_2011a .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This cyclic interplay of baryons in the CGM, often referred to as the ``baryon cycle'', is a key factor governing galaxy evolution \citep[e.g.,][]{Peroux2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: It is widely believed that these processes played a crucial role in determining the evolution of the cosmic star formation rate density (SFRD) which shows a peak at redshift of $z \approx 2-3$ and declines by almost an order of magnitude at both higher and lower redshifts \citep[e.g.,][]{Madau2014review, van_de_voort_2011b .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Given that hydrogen accounts for the majority of the baryonic matter in the universe, it has become a vital tool for probing the cool gas that acts as the fuel for star formation.
elements.sent-2411-018: Probing the cool gas in the CGM in emission is challenging owing to its low density ($n_{\rm H \sim 10^{-3}\, \rm cm^{-3}$ at $z\approx 3$).}
elements.sent-2411-019: State-of-the-art integral field spectrographs (IFS) such as VLT/MUSE \cite[Multi-Unit Spectroscopic Explorer;][]{bacon2010muse and Keck/KCWI \cite[Keck Cosmic Web Imager;][]{Morrissey_2018 have enabled the exploration of the diffuse medium surrounding galaxies in \lya\ emission within a few tens of kpc from normal galaxies \citep[e.g.,][]{Wisotzki2018, Erb2018, Guo2024a, Guo2024b and a few hundreds of pkpc around active galaxies \citep[e.g.,][]{Cantalupo2014,Borisova_2016, Fabrizio2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Extended \lya\ and metal line emission is also detected in several individual cases and in stacks \citep[e.g.,][]{Steidel2011, Zabl_2021,Johnson_2022,Leclercq2022,Rdutta2023,Guo2023_stack,Liu2024, Johnson2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the absorption spectra from bright background sources, such as quasars, remain the most sensitive tool to probe this elusive medium.
elements.sent-2411-022: Historically, the relation between galaxies and the neutral gas surrounding them was studied by identifying host-galaxies of strong \HI\ absorbers, such as Lyman-limit systems \citep[LLSs; \NHI$>10^{17.2}$~\sqcm; e.g.,][]{Lofthouse_2023 , and Damped Lyman-$\alpha$ absorbers \citep[DLAs; \NHI$\gtrsim10^{20.3}$~\sqcm; e.g.,][]{Peroux_2012, Krogager_2017, Mackenzie_2019 , seen in quasar spectra.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Several investigations have primarily focused on high-metallicity DLAs, driven by the presumption that these DLAs possess more luminous galaxy counterparts, owing to the underlying mass-metallicity relation \citep[e.g.,][] {Neeleman_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: These studies utilized facilities such as VLT/X-Shooter \citep[e.g.,][]{Krogager_2017 , VLT/SINFONI \citep[e.g.,][]{Peroux_2012 , and ALMA \citep[e.g.,][]{Neeleman2017, Neeleman2019 , revealing close associations (often within a few tens of kpc) between DLAs and their host galaxies.}}}

elements.sent-2411-074: The accurate measurement of \HI\ column densities in the spectra of high-$z$ quasars is challenging due to (a) the blending of \HI\ absorption with other \HI\ or metal lines present in the \lya\ forest and (b) the saturation of strong \HI\ absorption components.
elements.sent-2411-075: To overcome these challenges, it is imperative to simultaneously fit all the higher-order Lyman-series lines.
elements.sent-2411-076: This approach not only facilitates the accurate measurement of column density but also aids in effectively identifying contamination, thereby simplifying its removal.
elements.sent-2411-077: In our study, we conducted a detailed Voigt profile decomposition of \HI\ absorption detected within $\pm500$~\kms\ of the 96 MUSEQuBES LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-078: Note that a given absorber may be associated with multiple LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-079: For Voigt profile decomposition, we used the {\sc vpfit code \citep{Carswell2014 that works based on $\chi^2$-minimization.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: We obtained the best-fitting values for the redshift, Doppler parameter ($b$), and column density ($N$) of the $800$ associated individual \HI\ components in the redshift range $2.914 \leq z \leq 3.823$ (median $z=3.364$).
elements.sent-2411-081: The best-fitting parameters are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:catalog .}
elements.sent-2411-082: To ensure the reliability of our measurements, we manually examined the fitted profiles and repeated the fitting process until a satisfactory fit was achieved, in addition to the $\chi ^2$ and AICc (Akaike information criterion; corrected for small sample sizes) values of the model.

elements.subsection-2411-009: The relation between neutral gas in the ISM and CGM
elements.sent-2411-261: In Fig.~\ref{fig:fc_ew0 , we observed an anti-correlation between \fc(\HI) and \ew.}
elements.sent-2411-262: This trend is particularly evident for the threshold \logN~$=16.0$, where the difference in \fc(\HI) between the two \ew\ bins is significant at the $\approx 2\sigma$ level.
elements.sent-2411-263: Since $\approx 50\%$ of LAEs in both the \ew\ bins are associated with the pair/group subsample, it is unlikely that environmental effects are driving the trend.
elements.sent-2411-264: Simple shell-models of \lya\ radiative transfer predict a strong anti-correlation between $N(\HI)$ and \ew\ \citep[]{Verhamme2015, Verhamme2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-265: This is because as $N(\HI)$ increases, \lya\ photons experience more scattering, unlike continuum photons.
elements.sent-2411-266: Hence, \lya photons cover a longer distance in order to shift their frequencies off-resonance to escape the ISM of the galaxies, which increases their probability of being destroyed by dust grains.
elements.sent-2411-267: Thus, the observed anti-correlation between \fc(\HI) and \ew\ could imply a positive trend between the covering fraction of neutral gas in the CGM and $N(\HI)$ in the ISM of the LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-268: In other words, galaxies whose environments are abundant in cool gas are also inherently gas-rich.
elements.sent-2411-269: The shell model \citep[]{Verhamme2006 also predicts that galaxies with lower \NHI\ exhibit narrower line profiles, and vice versa.}
elements.sent-2411-270: However, we did not observe a significant difference in the distribution of FWHM of \lya\ emission lines between the high and low-\ew\ samples ($p_{\rm KS = 0.2$).}
elements.sent-2411-271: Both samples have a median FWHM of $\approx250$~\kms.
elements.sent-2411-272: An alternative explanation for the observed trend could be enhanced photoionization from the local radiation fields of LAEs with relatively higher \ew, which eventually leads to a lower \HI\ covering fraction \citep[e.g.,][]{Matthee2022 .}
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/plot_Tlya_paper.pdf} \caption{The mean excess-\lya-transmission ($\langle \delta_T \rangle$) as a function of 3D distance. The $\langle \delta_T \rangle$ values from this work (in orange) are compared with those at $z\approx 4.3$ from \citet{Matthee2024} (in blue). The error bars for our sample represent the $1 \sigma$ bootstrap error, calculated by resampling the LAE sample $1000$ times. The black dot-dash line indicates $\langle \delta_T \rangle=0$. } \label{fig:T_lya}
elements.sent-2411-273: In the current section, we discuss the influence of LAEs on the \lya\ transmission as measured in the quasar spectra.
elements.sent-2411-274: We define the excess \lya\ transmission ($\delta_T$) with respect to the average transmission at a given redshift (i.e., $\langle T_z \rangle$) as: \begin{equation}\delta_T = \frac{T}{\langle T_z \rangle} - 1~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-275: Here, $T$ represents the normalized spectral flux.
elements.sent-2411-276: Here, $r_{\parallel}$ and $r_{\bot}$ represent the LOS and transverse distances with respect to the LAEs, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-277: To calculate this, we summed the total flux from the normalized quasar spectra within the LOS velocity window $\Delta v$ around the systemic redshift of each LAE and averaged it over the total number of pixels contributing to the respective $\Delta v$ bin considering all LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-278: We converted $\Delta v$ to $r_{\parallel}$ (in comoving Mpc or cMpc) using $\frac{\Delta v {H(z) \times (1+z)$, assuming a redshift of $z=3.3$, the median for these LAEs.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: Additionally, we assumed $r_{\bot \approx 0.7$~cMpc, corresponding to the median impact parameter of 160~pkpc for our sample.}
elements.sent-2411-280: To model the redshift evolution of $\langle T_z \rangle$ in our data, we followed a method similar to \citet[][see also \citet{Becker_2015 ]{Matthee2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-281: We used the following polynomial function that best fits our data: \begin{equation}\langle T_z \rangle = -1.30 + 1.34\, z - 0.23\, z^2~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-282: We have also shown the $\langle \delta_T \rangle$ near the LAEs at a slightly higher redshift of $\approx 4.3$, as found by \citet{Matthee2024 (in blue).}
elements.sent-2411-283: The data points are mostly within each other's error bars.
elements.sent-2411-284: This work is a part of the MUSEQuBES survey at high-$z$ \citep[]{Muzahid_2020, Muzahid_2021 , which focuses on the CGM of high $z$ LAEs.}
elements.sent-2411-285: The MUSEQuBES sample consists of 96 LAEs (median \lya\ luminosity $\approx 10^{42}\, \rm erg\, s^{-1}$ and a median SFR of $\approx 1.3$~\Msun~$\rm yr^{-1}$, without accounting for dust extinction) within the redshift range $2.9$--$3.8$ that were detected in $8$ MUSE fields centered on $8$ bright background quasars.
elements.sent-2411-286: The high-resolution optical spectra of these $8$ quasars obtained with VLT/UVES and/or Keck/HIRES enabled us to explore the neutral hydrogen (\HI) absorption arising near these 96 galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-287: We estimated the systemic redshifts of the LAEs using the empirical relation given in \citet{Muzahid_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-288: Subsequently, we conducted a Voigt profile decomposition of the Lyman series lines detected within a velocity range of $\pm500$~\kms\ around the LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-289: The column densities of the 800 \HI\ absorption components observed in these spectra spans a range from \logN\ $=11.7 - 20.3$, with a median value of \logN\ $=13.7$.
elements.sent-2411-290: In cases where we were unable to precisely constrain the column density due to blending or the absence of higher-order unsaturated Lyman series lines, we reported the lower limit on the column density.
elements.sent-2411-292: We do not find any significant correlation between the total \NHI\ and impact parameter within 16--315~pkpc (median $165$~pkpc; Fig.~\ref{fig:col_profile ).}
elements.sent-2411-293: Nevertheless, the average total \NHI\ within $\pm500$~\kms\ of the LAEs exceeds the total \NHI\ within $\pm500$~\kms\ of random redshifts by an order of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-294: This is also true for the absorbers within $\pm250$~\kms\ of the LAE redshifts.
elements.sent-2411-295: These findings indicate that \HI\ absorption is enhanced near the LAEs compared to the IGM.
elements.sent-2411-296: This is likely due to the non-detection of the actual absorber hosts owing to their faintness, as discussed in Section \ref{sec:discussion_LLS .}
elements.sent-2411-297: The covering fraction, \fc(\HI), is consistently higher near the LAEs as compared to random regions for thresholds \logN~$=14-16$, with $\rho \leq 320$~pkpc.
elements.sent-2411-298: The relative enhancement in \fc(\HI) with respect to random regions increases with the threshold column density (Fig.~\ref{fig:fc ).}
elements.sent-2411-299: The pairs/group galaxies exhibit a higher \fc(\HI) compared to isolated LAEs for a threshold \logN$=15$ along transverse directions (Fig.~\ref{fig:fc_env_rho : {\it left ).}}
elements.sent-2411-300: The pairs/group LAEs display a $100\%$ \HI\ covering fraction, extending up to $\approx 250$~pkpc.
elements.sent-2411-301: The \fc(\HI) decreases with LOS velocity between the LAEs and absorbers (Fig.~\ref{fig:fc_env_rho , {\it right ).}}
elements.sent-2411-302: The trend of pairs/group having higher \fc(\HI) compared to isolated ones is also visible along the LOS-direction, although only upto $\approx \pm300$~\kms, beyond which \fc(\HI) values are comparable within $1\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-303: For a threshold \logN~$=16$, \fc(\HI) anti-correlates with the rest frame equivalent width (\ew) of \lya\ emission (Fig.~\ref{fig:fc_ew0 ).}
elements.sent-2411-304: According to the \lya\ shell model, a lower \ew\ results from a large reservoir of neutral gas present within the galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-305: This could imply that galaxies residing in cool-gas-rich environments are also inherently gas-rich.
elements.sent-2411-306: The other reason might be that high-\ew\ LAEs are efficient in ionizing their surrounding medium which decreases the \fc(\HI) (see Section~\ref{sec:discussion_ew ).}
elements.sent-2411-307: Finally, we compared the excess \lya\ transmission, $\langle \delta_T \rangle$, from our observation and that of the LAEs at slightly higher redshift of $4.3$ and found they are mostly consistent with each other.
elements.sent-2411-308: This study presents the connection between $z\approx 3$ LAEs and cool neutral gas in their surroundings, for the first time, in an ``absorption-blind'' manner using detailed Voigt profile decomposition of \HI\ absorbers.
elements.sent-2411-309: In the future, we will present ionization models to derive the density and metallicity of these absorbers and investigate their relation with the LAEs.
